Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered. I'd usually fit in studying around my other commitments, maybe a couple hours here and there when I could find the time. Being 28 and having other things going on in life, I couldn't always stick to a rigid schedule. I'm more of a hands-on learner, so I'd often prefer doing practice problems or working through examples rather than just reading. Some weeks I'd barely study at all, then cram more intensively when needed.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on deadlines and what else was happening in my life. I'd love to say I had a consistent weekly schedule, but that wasn't my reality. When assignments were due, I'd ramp up significantly, sometimes pulling late sessions. Other weeks, especially when nothing was immediately pressing, I'd drift and barely touch the course materials. It wasn't ideal, but I made it work.
Q3: Usually it was either checking if there was something due soon or seeing if grades had been posted. Sometimes I'd get an email notification about a new announcement and that would prompt me to log in. I wasn't someone who logged in just to browse around or stay connected with the course community. It was pretty transactional for me—get in, get what I need, get out.
Q4: I wouldn't say it ever became a natural routine for me. There were brief periods, maybe a week or two, where I'd check in more regularly because I was actively working on a major project. But once that pressure lifted, I'd drift back to logging in only when necessary. The LMS never really felt like a place I wanted to hang out in—it was more of a tool I used when I had to.
Q5: The practice exercises and any hands-on materials were the most useful for me because I learn by doing, not by reading or watching. I also found the assignment submission feedback helpful when it came through quickly. Videos were okay if they demonstrated something practical, like working through a problem step by step. Anything I could actively engage with rather than passively consume worked better for how my brain operates.
Q6: Discussion forums I mostly ignored—I only posted three times the whole course, and that was probably when it was required. I just didn't feel like those conversations added much to my understanding. Some of the supplementary readings I'd skip over too, especially if they seemed theoretical rather than practical. If something didn't feel immediately applicable to assignments or exams, I'd often deprioritize it.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to my grade or to actually understanding something I'd need later. Practical application was key for me. If I finished something and felt like I'd actually learned a skill or could solve a problem I couldn't before, that was valuable. Abstract discussions or busy work that felt like box-checking? Those felt like a waste.
Q8: Optional activities always felt like they were competing with everything else demanding my attention. If something was required, I'd make sure to do it regardless of how I felt. But optional stuff? I'd only engage if I had extra time and energy, which wasn't often given my anxiety levels and other responsibilities. Required activities had urgency; optional ones just had potential value that rarely won out.
Q9: Absolutely. I'd download PDFs and work through them on my tablet away from the computer. I also used physical notebooks a lot—writing things out by hand helps me process information better than typing. Sometimes I'd find YouTube videos or external resources that explained concepts differently than the course materials. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the LMS entirely.
Q10: Many evenings I'd be working through problems in my notebook or reviewing printed materials while the TV was on in the background. That's my preferred study environment, but obviously none of that shows up anywhere. I'd also talk through concepts with a friend who's in a similar field—those conversations were really valuable for solidifying my understanding, but there's no log of that.
Q11: Yeah, this was pretty common for me. I'd watch a video once to get the gist, then download any accompanying materials and work through them offline multiple times. Or I'd screenshot key slides and review them on my phone during downtime. The LMS would show one view, but I might have studied that content five or six times in different contexts.
Q12: Mid-semester was rough. My anxiety was really spiking, and I was dealing with some personal stuff that made it hard to focus. There were a couple weeks where I'd log in, stare at the screen, and just feel overwhelmed by everything I needed to do. The mindfulness exercises they offered helped somewhat, but there were definitely periods where engaging felt almost impossible despite knowing I needed to.
Q13: Definitely. When I was stressed or fatigued, I'd do the bare minimum on the LMS—just enough to not fall behind. My anxiety would sometimes make me avoid logging in altogether because seeing all the tasks would feel overwhelming. Competing responsibilities from life outside university meant the LMS often got deprioritized until deadlines forced my hand. It wasn't about not caring; it was about capacity.
Q14: Oh, plenty of times. I'd have a video playing while my mind was completely elsewhere, thinking about other problems in my life. Or I'd be scrolling through readings without actually absorbing anything because my anxiety was too high to concentrate. Those sessions would show up as engagement in the data, but I'd have to redo everything later when I was in a better headspace.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts constantly because they felt low-stakes and anxiety-inducing at the same time—having to put my thoughts out there for others to see. I'd also delay starting big assignments even though I knew an early start would reduce my stress. It's like my brain knew what was smart but my anxiety would create this avoidance pattern anyway. The mindfulness stuff helped me recognize this, at least.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my external motivation system. Without them, I'd drift. With them, I'd focus intensely in the final days or hours before something was due. My LMS activity probably showed clear spikes around due dates and relative quiet in between. It's not the healthiest pattern, but when you're juggling life at 28 with high anxiety, sometimes that's just how it goes.
Q17: Getting good feedback was actually really motivating—it helped calm my anxiety about whether I was on the right track. When feedback came quickly, I'd engage more with the next task because I felt confident. But when feedback was delayed or vague, I'd kind of disengage and second-guess everything. My high assignment scores helped build confidence over time, which made it easier to keep going.
Q18: The course structure gave me enough flexibility that I could work around my inconsistent schedule, which I appreciated. Having clear deadlines helped me prioritize. But the structure also meant I could coast for a while and then catch up, which fed into my drifting tendencies. A more rigid structure might have kept me more consistently engaged, though it also might have increased my anxiety.
Q19: The hands-on components and practical assignments made engagement easier because they matched my learning style. What made it harder was when there was too much reading or when discussion participation felt forced. Also, the sheer volume of content in 15 online courses meant I had to constantly triage what to focus on. Sometimes the course design assumed more consistent engagement than I could realistically give.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is anxious or overwhelmed—they're logging in, trying to engage, but can't actually complete work because they're stuck or stressed. Or maybe they're searching for something specific and can't find it, so they keep clicking around. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity. Sometimes it's just someone struggling to figure out what they're supposed to be doing.
Q21: That actually describes me pretty well. I performed at distinction level despite not being super active in the LMS because I did a lot of learning offline. I'd download materials, study from my notes, work through problems independently. The LMS activity doesn't capture someone sitting with a notebook for three hours working through concepts. Efficient, focused offline study can absolutely coexist with low online visibility.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you're active, what you're clicking on, whether you're meeting deadlines. That's useful data. But they completely miss the quality of engagement, the offline work, the mental state behind the clicks. They can't tell if someone watched a video attentively or had it playing while zoned out. They miss the thinking, the struggling, the real learning that often happens away from the screen.
Q23: For me, being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just going through the motions to get a grade. It's when I'm actively thinking, questioning, applying concepts to real situations. It's feeling connected to why the content matters, even when it's hard. Engagement isn't about how many times I logged in—it's about whether my brain was actually working on the material.
Q24: The anxiety I dealt with throughout the course wouldn't show up at all, nor would the mindfulness practices I used to manage it. My offline study habits, the conversations with friends, the times I was genuinely wrestling with concepts in my head while doing other things—none of that appears. The data would show a somewhat disengaged student who still got good grades, but it wouldn't show the internal effort and struggle behind those results.
